script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Thursday — here is your QQQ recap. The QQQ portfolio closed one point five six percent down on the session. It was a bruising day for semiconductor names, and the damage was concentrated enough to pull the broader portfolio into the red despite pockets of real strength elsewhere.

Intel fell nearly ten percent in a single session. That is not a rounding error — that is a company shedding close to one tenth of its market value in a single trading day, and for a name already carrying the weight of a multi-year turnaround story, Thursday's move raises serious questions about near-term execution and investor patience.

No macro news was available for Thursday's session. Markets appeared to trade on company-specific catalysts and sector rotation dynamics, with semiconductor names bearing the brunt of selling pressure while select large-cap technology and consumer names held their ground. The Nasdaq Composite finished the session lower, reflecting the outsized drag from chip-linked positions across the index.

The top story out of Thursday's session belongs to the semiconductor space, and it is a wide lens. Intel, Micron Technology, Advanced Micro Devices, and Applied Materials — a secondary holding — all posted significant single-session losses, raising a pointed question heading into earnings season: is the AI chip trade entering a more pronounced correction phase? Intel's drop of nine point seven nine percent was the headline number, but Advanced Micro Devices falling six point two eight percent and Micron Technology declining three point six five percent tells a consistent story of sector-wide pressure. Zacks Investment Ideas flagged Micron and Qualcomm in a Thursday research note, pointing to the tensions between strong longer-term AI infrastructure demand and near-term sector pullback dynamics. The Omdia research group separately reported that the worldwide personal computer market declined three point six percent year over year in the second quarter of twenty twenty-six, landing at sixty-five point seven million units shipped — a data point that adds further headwind context for hardware-adjacent semiconductor names heading into the next earnings cycle.

Turning to the holdings rundown, beginning with the portfolio's largest position. Nvidia closed at two hundred four dollars and twelve cents, up four point three eight percent on Thursday's session, making it the standout performer among the primary nine. While much of the semiconductor space sold off, Nvidia moved in the opposite direction — a divergence worth noting. Yahoo Finance flagged that many high-flying artificial intelligence stocks have officially entered correction territory, and yet Nvidia's Thursday move suggests the market continues to treat it as a category unto itself within the AI infrastructure trade. Watch for any updated analyst price target revisions heading into earnings season.

Apple closed at three hundred thirteen dollars and thirty-nine cents, up zero point two three percent on the session — a quiet but positive close in a difficult tape. The most relevant Apple headline Thursday had little to do with the company's own operations: Broadcom extended its Apple chip supply agreement through twenty thirty-one in a deal valued at approximately thirty billion dollars, locking in a key supplier relationship across multiple generations of Apple silicon. Separately, the Omdia personal computer market decline of three point six percent year over year is a modest headwind for Apple's Mac line. The primary catalyst to monitor remains the broader earnings season setup.

Alphabet closed at three hundred sixty-one dollars and ninety-two cents, down one point two four percent Thursday. The name appeared in several broad market roundups, including a Zacks feature spotlighting Alphabet alongside Nvidia and Amazon in the context of AI infrastructure demand. Yahoo Finance also flagged Thursday's session as a significant test for large-cap technology names ahead of second-quarter earnings reports. Alphabet's pullback was contained relative to the semiconductor group, but the communication services sector warrants continued attention as earnings dates approach.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred forty-eight dollars and eighty cents, down three point six five percent on Thursday. The Zacks morning note specifically highlighted Micron alongside Qualcomm, acknowledging AI infrastructure demand as a long-term driver while noting the near-term sector pullback dynamic. Micron remains a high-beta name within the portfolio — its move Thursday was directionally consistent with broad semiconductor weakness. Upcoming earnings guidance will be the next meaningful data point for this position.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-three dollars and thirty-four cents, down zero point eight eight percent Thursday. No specific Microsoft news surfaced during the session. The decline was modest relative to the broader semiconductor selloff and reflected general large-cap technology softness rather than company-specific pressure. The cloud and enterprise software earnings cycle is the next key event to monitor.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-three dollars and sixty-two cents, down zero point two two percent on Thursday — an essentially flat session for the consumer discretionary and cloud giant. Amazon appeared in a Zacks investment note Thursday in the context of AI infrastructure positioning. No company-specific catalysts emerged. The e-commerce and Amazon Web Services earnings reports remain the primary data points ahead.

Over at Advanced Micro Devices, the session was sharply negative. Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred seventeen dollars and forty cents, down six point two eight percent Thursday — one of the steepest single-session losses among the primary holdings. No company-specific news drove the move; this appears tied to broad semiconductor sector pressure. Advanced Micro Devices is a high-volatility name within the portfolio, and its Thursday decline extended what has been a difficult stretch for the AI chip trade broadly. Earnings visibility will be critical.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and six cents, down six point one two percent on Thursday. Yahoo Finance noted Thursday that Tesla investors will need to exercise patience with the robotaxi rollout, while better-than-expected delivery figures have provided some support to the longer-term investment case. The tension between near-term execution risk on autonomous vehicles and the delivery beat narrative is the central story for Tesla holders. The next major catalyst is any updated timeline communication on the robotaxi program.

Intel closed at one hundred ten dollars and twenty-four cents, down nine point seven nine percent — the worst single-session performance among the primary nine and the worst in the portfolio on Thursday. No single headline explained the full magnitude of the move, but the drop lands Intel in a precarious position technically and fundamentally. The company's multi-year turnaround remains a work in progress, and a nearly ten percent single-session decline reflects how thin investor patience has grown. Any forward guidance update or manufacturing milestone announcement becomes the key watch item for this position.

Three data points and events to watch over the next forty-eight hours. First, any Federal Reserve communication from Chair Kevin Warsh — Yahoo Finance flagged Thursday that Warsh may have signaled directional intent on interest rates, which carries direct implications for rate-sensitive technology valuations across the portfolio. Second, the opening of second-quarter earnings season for major technology names — specifically any early reporters among the Nasdaq-heavy cohort, as Yahoo Finance flagged Thursday's session as the market's biggest test ahead of earnings. Third, continued semiconductor sector pricing action — given Thursday's broad losses across Intel, Advanced Micro Devices, Micron, and the secondary name Applied Materials, Friday's opening tone in chip stocks will signal whether institutional sellers are finished or still active.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Keep close watch on the semiconductor sector — Friday's price action will tell us whether Thursday's selloff was a flush or the beginning of something more sustained.
